title: Progression-free sets in $\mathbb{Z}_4^n$ are exponentially small

thm:1.summary: If $A \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_4^n$ is progression-free (no $a,b,c \in A$ with $a + c = 2b$, $a \ne b$), then $|A| \le 4^{\gamma n}$ where $\gamma := \max_{0 < \varepsilon < 1/4} \frac{1}{2}(H(1/2 - \varepsilon) + H(2\varepsilon)) \approx 0.926$ and $H$ is the binary entropy. Breaks the $\mathbb{Z}_4^n$ analog of the cap-set problem exponentially: the previous bound was the trivial $\sim 4^n / n$ from Meshulam-type Fourier arguments.

cor:1.summary: If $G$ is a finite abelian group and $n = \mathrm{rk}_4(G)$ is the 4-rank of $G$, then $r_3(G) \le 4^{-(1-\gamma) n} |G|$ where $r_3(G)$ is the largest size of a progression-free subset. Lifts thm:1 from $\mathbb{Z}_4^n$ to general abelian groups via the 4-rank, providing exponential savings whenever the group has enough $\mathbb{Z}_4$ structure.

lem:1.summary: Polynomial-method core. Let $P$ be a multilinear polynomial in $n$ variables over a field $\mathbb{F}$ of total degree at most $d$, and $A \subseteq \mathbb{F}^n$ with $|A| > 2 \sum_{0 \le i \le d/2} \binom{n}{i}$. If $P(a - b) = 0$ for all $a \ne b \in A$, then $P(0) = 0$. The novel ingredient: factoring $P(x-y) = \langle u(x), v(y) \rangle$ for vectors $u, v \in \mathbb{F}^{2m}$ where $m = \sum_{0 \le i \le d/2} \binom{n}{i}$, so a large pairwise-vanishing set forces $P$ to vanish at $0$ by rank counting.

prop:1.summary: Coset distribution bound. If $A \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_4^n$ is progression-free, then for every $0 < \varepsilon < 1/4$, the number of $F_n$-cosets (where $F_n \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_4^n$ is the subgroup of elements of order dividing $2$) containing at least $2^{n H(1/2 - \varepsilon) + 1}$ elements of $A$ is less than $2^{n H(2\varepsilon)}$. Applies lem:1 with $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{F}_2$ inside each coset: progression-freeness forces sums $2 \cdot A_R$ to miss the doubled coset representatives $2 \ast R$, and the polynomial-vanishing dichotomy gives the count.

proof:lem:1.summary: Proof of lem:1: expand $P(x-y) = \sum_{I,J} C_{I,J} x^I y^J$ over disjoint $I, J$ with $|I| + |J| \le d$, split into $|I| \le d/2$ and $|J| \le d/2$ halves to write $P(x-y) = \langle u(x), v(y) \rangle$ with $u, v \in \mathbb{F}^{2m}$. If $|A| > 2m$, the $|A| \times |A|$ matrix $[P(a-b)]_{a,b \in A}$ has rank at most $2m < |A|$, so its non-singular pairwise-vanishing pattern is impossible unless the diagonal vanishes.

rem:1.summary: lem:1 extends from multilinear to bounded-individual-degree polynomials: if every individual degree of $P$ is at most $\delta$, replace $\sum_{i \le d/2} \binom{n}{i}$ with $f_\delta(n, \lfloor d/2 \rfloor)$ (the count of monomials with degrees bounded by $\delta$ and total degree at most $d$). Taking $\delta = |\mathbb{F}| - 1$ for finite $\mathbb{F}$ removes the individual-degree assumption entirely — the form Ellenberg–Gijswijt use in $\mathbb{F}_3^n$.

proof:prop:1.summary: Proof of prop:1: let $\mathcal{R}$ be the cosets containing many elements of $A$, $A_R = A \cap R$. Progression-freeness implies $B = \cup_R (2 \cdot A_R)$ and $C = \cup_R (2 \ast R)$ are disjoint subsets of $F_n$ (else a 3-AP exists). With $d = n - \lceil 2\varepsilon n \rceil$, the size bound gives $|A_R| > 2 \sum_{i \le d/2} \binom{n}{i}$, so lem:1 applies to the indicator polynomial of $A_R - A_R$ and forces $|\mathcal{R}| < 2^{n H(2\varepsilon)}$ via the disjointness.

proof:thm:1.summary: Proof of thm:1: integrate prop:1 over $\varepsilon \in (0, 1/4)$. Write $|A| = \int_0^{2^{n+1}} N(x) \, dx$ where $N(x)$ counts cosets with at least $x$ elements; split at $x = 2^{n H(1/4) + 1}$, bound the low-$x$ part trivially and the high-$x$ part via prop:1 to get $|A| < (n+2) \cdot 4^{\gamma n}$. Tensor-power trick (apply to $A^k \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_4^{kn}$) removes the polynomial factor.

edges.note: Integrates the coset-distribution bound prop:1 over $\varepsilon$ to get the global size bound on $A$.
Polynomial-method machinery flows through prop:1 to thm:1; lem:1 is the rank-counting heart.
Quotients $G$ by the maximal subgroup of odd order to reduce to the $\mathbb{Z}_4^n$ case.
Applies lem:1 to the difference-set polynomial inside each $F_n$-coset; the size threshold $2^{n H(1/2 - \varepsilon) + 1}$ is exactly the lem:1 bound on $|A_R|$.
Proof of lem:1 — the polynomial-tensor-decomposition / rank argument.
Proof of prop:1 — applies lem:1 to the coset structure.
Proof of thm:1 — integrates prop:1 and tensor-powers.
Generalizes lem:1 to bounded-individual-degree polynomials; this is the form used downstream by Ellenberg–Gijswijt for $\mathbb{F}_3^n$.

polynomial_lemma.description: The rank-counting core: a multilinear polynomial $P$ that vanishes on all pairwise differences $a - b$ of a sufficiently large set $A$ must also vanish at $0$.

coset_argument.description: Reduce the global $\mathbb{Z}_4^n$ problem to a $\mathbb{F}_2^n$-coset count: progression-freeness inside any heavy coset forces a polynomial constraint that lem:1 vetoes.

integration_and_tensor_trick.description: Integrate the per-$\varepsilon$ coset count, then remove polynomial factors via the tensor-power trick to get the clean exponential bound.

extension_to_abelian_groups.description: Lift from $\mathbb{Z}_4^n$ to general finite abelian groups via the 4-rank.

b:m.title: Meshulam — On subsets of finite abelian groups with no 3-term arithmetic progression

b:b.title: Behrend — On sets of integers which contain no three terms in arithmetical progression

b:fgr.title: Frankl, Graham, Rödl — On subsets of abelian groups with no 3-term arithmetic progression
